course_title: Knowledge and Evidence in Public Policy

learning_outcomes.description: Identify the different forms of knowledge and how they variously contribute to policy debates and policy innovation.
Critically assess “problem framing” in the construction of particular policy strategies and documents.
Critically assess how various forms of knowledge and evidence are used in the development of Reports by public inquiries.
Analyse the processes used for policy evaluation and review.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to attend and participate in seminars. For this purpose, marks will be awarded for active participation in group discussions during the seminar.

Discussions will consider key concepts/frameworks, and their application to a wide variety of real-world policy issues.

Assessment tasks will also be workshopped in class.

Marks will be awarded for active participation in (any) 10 of the 11 available seminars. 

If students have a genuine reason for not being able to attend a seminar class, an alternative substitute task may be assigned for rapid completion, such as a brief commentary on a core reading.

This assessment item is completed in-person.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Your first short Policy Knowledge Analysis will analyse a policy problem that you think would benefit from better use of available knowledge or evidence.

In discussing current shortfalls in the policy itself and/or shortfalls in the policy debate, explain why/how more attention to evidence or different types of knowledge could improve the potential outcomes for this policy issue.

You must refer to at least 10 academic or governmental references including items that are specifically core reading for this course.

The relevant literature and your chosen policy example should be clarified through conversation with the course convenor (internal students) or seminar leader (external students).

The assignment will be submitted via Turnitin.

A 10% tolerance (plus or minus 10%) on the word limit is permitted.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. However, students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
Short Plan (2-3 pages) due on 9/10/2026 5:00 pm

Policy Research Brief due on 30/10/2026 5:00 pm
assessments.task_description:
This task consists of two components.

1.You must first submit a 2-3-page Plan via Turnitin by the end of Week 10 (Friday 9th October 2026). Please consider early prior discussion of your ideas for the Plan with either the course convenor (for internal students) or your online seminar leader (for external students).

This is a mandatory identity-verified pass/fail hurdle. Early submission of the Plan is encouraged. An extension of time may be applied for through the standard administrative channels.

The Plan will outline the proposed topic of your research, it will outline your overall analytical argument, it will provide justifications for your choice of at least ten key references, and it will outline your essay structure. In other words, you must outline how you propose to tackle the conceptual and empirical research challenges.

This short Plan (2-3 pages) will be discussed and defended in an oral conversation of 10 minutes with the course convenor or the online seminar leader. You will receive immediate feedback, and a pass/fail outcome will be documented. Resubmission of a revised version of the Plan is permitted to achieve a pass outcome. Success with this planning hurdle will enable you to finalise your approach for writing the Policy Research Brief.

If you do not submit and pass this mandatory hurdle (the Plan), your Policy Research Brief cannot be assessed.

2. Your Policy Research Brief is 3500 words (including References), with a 10% tolerance (plus or minus 10%) being permitted. It is due at the end of week 13 (30 October 2026).

Here is the task for the major Policy Research Brief:

Select a policy issue that has been the subject of a recent major Report or Inquiry. 

Consider how knowledge/evidence have been used in the construction of the Report and/or in the policy debate surrounding this policy issue.

In other words, consider how the use of knowledge/evidence has shaped the argument in the report.

You may select an Australian or an international example, but you must ensure that all sources on which you rely have been clearly disclosed and that these sources should be available for checking by the assessor of the Policy Research Brief.

You must ensure that you analyse and utilise relevant academic and/or governmental references including key items from the core readings for this course.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 1 - Evidence and the policy process

This topic provides an overview of the course content, activities and assessment. It will also introduce some basic concepts and typologies for thinking about knowledge and evidence in public policy to be utilised throughout the course. Types of 'expertise' will be considered. Differences will be explored between research knowledge, stakeholder knowledge, and policymaker knowledge.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Topic 1 - Evidence and the policy process This topic provides an overview of the course content, activities and assessment. It will also introduce some basic concepts and typologies for thinking about knowledge and evidence in public policy to be utilised throughout the course. Types of 'expertise' will be considered. Differences will be explored between research knowledge, stakeholder knowledge, and policymaker knowledge.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 2 - Evidence-based policy and its critics

This topic will consider the classic rationalist v post-rationalist debate. It will outline 'rationalist' and data-driven approaches to knowledge and evidence in policy processes, and compare this with pragmatic approaches. We will then turn to look at the epistemology (theory of knowledge) that underpins calls for "evidence-based" policymaking, and explore more pragmatic alternatives including "evidence-informed" policymaking.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Topic 2 - Evidence-based policy and its critics This topic will consider the classic rationalist v post-rationalist debate. It will outline 'rationalist' and data-driven approaches to knowledge and evidence in policy processes, and compare this with pragmatic approaches. We will then turn to look at the epistemology (theory of knowledge) that underpins calls for "evidence-based" policymaking, and explore more pragmatic alternatives including "evidence-informed" policymaking.

learning_activities.topic:
No Class due to Public Holiday on Wed 12 Aug. A pre-recorded lecture available for Topic 3

This Topic 3 is Knowledge and evidence in policy debate (1). It considers how policy paradigms influence the use of knowledge and evidence.

We note the knowledge and evidence found in evaluation reports, and we consider how we might go about institutionalising evidence use.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_title: No Class due to Public Holiday on Wed 12 Aug. A pre-recorded lecture available for Topic 3

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 4 - Knowledge and evidence in policy debate (2)

The seminar discussion will be the first of three where we apply concepts and theories from the theoretical section of the course to some concrete public policy case studies. We will focus on the different kinds of knowledge and evidence used in various policy areas. We will focus on how the current approaches are 'framed' and what conditions and resources could help shift the established ways of thinking.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

We will focus on how the current approaches are 'framed' and what conditions and resources could help shift the established ways of thinking.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 5 - Knowledge & evidence for applied policy problems

This topic develops further examples of how to apply the concepts and theories from the theoretical section of the course to some concrete public policy case studies. We will focus on how the current policy approaches are 'framed' and what conditions and resources could help shift the established ways of thinking.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 6 - Knowledge & evidence for applied policy problems

This topic demonstrates how to apply the concepts and theories from the theoretical section of the course to policy practices. We will focus on the different kinds of knowledge and evidence used in several different tasks or contexts by practitioners -- policy advice, monitoring and coordination, evaluation and implementation.

Topic 8 - Knowledge and evidence for applied policy problems

This topic explores examples where we apply the concepts and theories of the course to policy practices and decision-making. This session will consider the uses of knowledge and evidence in policy evaluation.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 9 - Knowledge & evidence in applied policy areas

This topic continues to analyse policy examples where we apply the concepts and theories of the course to policy practices and decision-making. This session will continue to consider the use of knowledge and evidence in POLICY EVALUATION.

It is recommended that students complete their mandatory pass/fail Research Essay PLAN before the end of Week 10.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 10 - Knowledge & evidence for future challenges

This topic further discusses applied examples of contentious policy issues, and discussion of major Policy Research Brief topics.

Consideration of 'futuring' and forecasting as techniques for addressing policy uncertainties.
learning_activities.topic_description: Topic 10 - Knowledge & evidence for future challenges This topic further discusses applied examples of contentious policy issues, and discussion of major Policy Research Brief topics. Consideration of 'futuring' and forecasting as techniques for addressing policy uncertainties.

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 11 - Knowledge & evidence under threat

This topic considers the modern crisis undermining trust in politicians and trust in experts.

Is trust the foundation for evidence-informed policy debates and decision-making? Are experts part of the solution or part of the problem?
learning_activities.topic_description: Topic 11 - Knowledge & evidence under threat This topic considers the modern crisis undermining trust in politicians and trust in experts. Is trust the foundation for evidence-informed policy debates and decision-making? Are experts part of the solution or part of the problem?

learning_activities.topic:
Topic 12 - Course overview and wrap-up

This session will provide a discussion of the course themes and reflect on our collective insights as students of public policy. In the seminar we will look at evidence, innovation and policy change and ask how the use of knowledge and evidence could be improved.
